title: Tangents of circles problems
description: Solve problems related to tangents of circles.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angle $A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

**What is the measure of $\angle A$?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
A circumscribed angle is supplementary to the central angle that intercepts the same arc.

The reason is that $ABOC$ is a quadrilateral, so its angles add up to $360^\circ$.   Each of the tangent angles, $\angle ABO$ and $\angle ACO$, has a measure of $90^\circ$.

$\begin{align} 
\text{m}\angle ABO + \text{m}\angle ACO + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
90 + 90  + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 180\\
\end{align}$
$\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{92^\circ}= 180^\circ$
$\pink{\text{m}\angle A} = \pink{88^\circ}$

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment C O and segment C B are both radii. There are tangent lines from points C and B on the circle, making segment C A and segment A B. The four lines create quadrilateral C O B A, where angle C O B is ninety-two degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angle $A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

**What is the measure of $\angle O$?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
A circumscribed angle is supplementary to the central angle that intercepts the same arc.

The reason is that $ABOC$ is a quadrilateral, so its angles add up to $360^\circ$.   Each of the tangent angles, $\angle B$ and $\angle C$, has a measure of $90^\circ$.

>$\begin{align} 
\text{m}\angle B+ \text{m}\angle C+ \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
90 + 90  + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 180\\
\end{align}$
>$\pink{70^\circ} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O}= 180^\circ$
>$\blue{\text{m}\angle O} = \blue{110^\circ}$

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment C O and segment B O are both radii. There are tangent lines from points C and B on the circle, making segment C A and segment A B. The four lines create quadrilateral C O B A, where angle C A B is seventy degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angle $A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

**What is the measure of $\angle O$?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
A circumscribed angle is supplementary to the central angle that intercepts the same arc.

The reason is that $ABOC$ is a quadrilateral, so its angles add up to $360^\circ$.   Each of the tangent angles, $\angle ABO$ and $\angle ACO$, has a measure of $90^\circ$.

>$\begin{align} 
\text{m}\angle ABO + \text{m}\angle ACO + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
90 + 90  + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O} &= 180\\
\end{align}$
>$\pink{46^\circ} + \blue{\text{m}\angle O}= 180^\circ$
>$\blue{\text{m}\angle O} = \blue{134^\circ}$

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment C O and segment B O are both radii. There are tangent lines from points C and B on the circle, making segment C A and segment A B. The four lines create quadrilateral C O B A, where angle C A B is forty-six degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angle $A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

**What is the measure of $\angle D$?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints:
A circumscribed angle is supplementary to the central angle that intercepts the same arc.

The reason is that $ABOC$ is a quadrilateral, so its angles add up to $360^\circ$.   Each of the tangent angles, $\angle ABO$ and $\angle ACO$, has a measure of $90^\circ$.

$\begin{align} 
\text{m}\angle ABO + \text{m}\angle ACO + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \green{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
90 + 90  + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \green{\text{m}\angle O} &= 360\\
\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \green{\text{m}\angle O} &= 180\\
\pink{80^\circ} + \green{\text{m}\angle O} &= 180^\circ\\
 \green{\text{m}\angle O} &= 100^\circ
\end{align}$
If the measure of central angle $\green{\angle O}$ is $\green{100^\circ}$, what is the measure of inscribed $\angle D$?

The measure of a central angle is equal to the measure of the arc it intercepts.  If $\green{\text{m}\angle O} = \green{100^\circ}$, then $\text{m}\stackrel{\LARGE{\frown}}{BC} = 100^\circ$.

The measure of an inscribed angle is half of the measure of the arc it intercepts.  If $\text{m}\stackrel{\LARGE{\frown}}{BC} = 100^\circ$, then $\blue{\text{m}\angle D} = \blue{50^\circ}$.
$\blue{\text{m}\angle D} = \blue{50^\circ}$

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Point A is outside of the circle. Segment O B and segment O C are both radii of the circle. There are line segments that connect point A to points B and C, creating segment A B and segment A C. Angle B A C measures eighty degrees. There is a point D on the circle that lies opposite to point A. There are chords connecting points B and C to point D, creating segment B D and segment C D.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angle $A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

**What is the measure of $\angle A$?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: If the measure of inscribed $\blue{\angle D}$ is $\blue{48^\circ}$, what is the measure of central $\angle O$?

The measure of an inscribed angle is half of the measure of the arc it intercepts.  If $\blue{\text{m}\angle D} = \blue{48^\circ}$, then $\text{m}\stackrel{\LARGE{\frown}}{BC} = 96^\circ$.

The measure of a central angle is equal to the measure of the arc it intercepts.  If $\text{m}\stackrel{\LARGE{\frown}}{BC} = 96^\circ$, then $\text{m}\angle O = 96^\circ$.

A circumscribed angle is supplementary to the central angle that intercepts the same arc.

The reason is that $ABOC$ is a quadrilateral, so its angles add up to $360^\circ$.   Each of the tangent angles, $\angle ABO$ and $\angle ACO$, has a measure of $90^\circ$.

$\begin{align} 
\text{m}\angle ABO + \text{m}\angle ACO + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \text{m}\angle O &= 360\\
90 + 90  + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \text{m}\angle O &= 360\\
\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \text{m}\angle O &= 180\\
\end{align}$
$\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + 96^\circ= 180^\circ$
$\pink{\text{m}\angle A} = \pink{84^\circ}$

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Point A is outside of the circle. Segment O B and segment O C are both radii of the circle. There are line segments that connect point A to points B and C, creating segment A B and segment A C. There is a point D on the circle that lies opposite to point A. There are chords connecting points B and C to point D, creating segment B D and segment C D. The angle B D C measures forty-eight degrees.

The measure of a central angle is equal to the measure of the arc it intercepts.  If $\text{m}\stackrel{\LARGE{\frown}}{BC} = 130^\circ$, then $\text{m}\angle O = 130^\circ$.

A circumscribed angle is supplementary to the central angle that intercepts the same arc.

The reason is that $ABOC$ is a quadrilateral, so its angles add up to $360^\circ$.   Each of the tangent angles, $\angle ABO$ and $\angle ACO$, has a measure of $90^\circ$.

$\begin{align} 
\text{m}\angle ABO + \text{m}\angle ACO + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \text{m}\angle O &= 360\\
90 + 90  + \pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \text{m}\angle O &= 360\\
\pink{\text{m}\angle A} + \text{m}\angle O &= 180\\

assessment_items.question_content:
$\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to circle $O$ at point $C$. 

**What is the measure of $\angle ACO$?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Because $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent the circle at point $C$, the radius going to point $C$ is perpendicular to the tangent line.
The measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a tangent line to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Angle O A C is sixteen degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]

The measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a line that could potentially be tangent to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Side A O is broken into two line segments, A B and B O. Angle B A C is twenty-eight degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to circle $O$ at point $C$. 

**What is the measure of $\angle O$?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] $^\circ$



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Because $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent the circle at point $C$, the radius going to point $C$ is perpendicular to the tangent line.  This means that the measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

We know that the angles of triangle $AOC$ have to add up to $180^\circ$.

$\begin{align}
\blue{\text{m}\angle OAC} + \pink{\text{m}\angle O} + \text{m}\angle OCA &= 180\\
\blue{38}+ \pink{\text{m}\angle O} + 90 &= 180 \\
\pink{\text{m}\angle O} + 128&= 180 \\
\pink{\text{m}\angle O} &= 52\\
\end{align}$
The measure of $\angle O$ is $52^\circ$.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a line that could potentially be tangent to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Side A O is broken into two line segments, A B and B O. Angle B A C is thirty-eight degrees.

assessment_items.question_content:
Angle $A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

**What is the length of $\overline{AB}$?**  
[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Any two segments tangent to a circle from a common endpoint are congruent.
Segments $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{AB}$ are both tangent to circle $O$  because $\angle A$ is circumscribed about circle $O$.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment C O and segment B O are both radii. There are tangent lines from points C and B on the circle, making segment C A and segment A B. The four lines create quadrilateral C O B A, where angle C A B is seventy-three degrees. Segment A C is four units. Segment C O is three units.

answers.message: This is the length of AO.  Any two segments tangent to a circle from a common endpoint are congruent.

There are chords connecting points B and C to point D, creating segment B D and segment C D. Angle B D C measures sixty-five degrees. Segment C D is five units. Segment O B is three units. Segment A B is six units.

assessment_items.question_content:
All sides of quadrilateral $ABCD$ are tangent to circle $P$.

**What is the perimeter of the quadrilateral below?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Any two segments tangent to a circle from a common endpoint are congruent.

With this information, we can identify two other segments which are also $4$ units long.

>[[☃ image 1]]

By the same logic as above, we can label the rest of the lengths in the perimeter.

>[[☃ image 1]]
>$\text{Perimeter}=4+8+\ldots=\,?$
The perimeter of the quadrilateral is $42$ units long.

options.alt: A circle centered around point P. The circle is inscribed inside kite A B C D so that each side is tangent to the circle. Side A B is twelve units. Side C D is nine units. From the tangent point on side B C to point C is four units. From the tangent point on side A D to point A is four units.

answers.message: This is the sum of the given lengths.  Use the fact that any two segments tangent to a circle from a common endpoint are congruent to find the missing side lengths.
This double counts some of the side lengths.  Be sure to add the length of each side only once.

assessment_items.question_content:
All sides of $\triangle ABC$ are tangent to circle $P$.

**What is the perimeter of the triangle below?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units

>[[☃ image 1]]

[Harder version, requires minor algebraic reasoning.

web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/f965949ceb8d6e18a92c062ee0b635e68d36caec]:
hints: Any two segments tangent to a circle from a common endpoint are congruent.

With this information, we can identify two other segments which are also $10$ and $5$ units long respectively.

>[[☃ image 1]]

By the same logic as above, we can label the rest of the lengths in the perimeter.

>[[☃ image 1]]
>$\text{Perimeter}=10 + 14+\ldots=\,?$
The perimeter of the triangle is $58$ units long.

options.alt: A circle centered around point P. The circle is inscribed inside triangle A B C so that each side is tangent to the circle. Side A B is twenty-four units. From the tangent point on side A C to point A is ten units. From the tangent point on side B C to point C is five units.

answers.message: This is the sum of the given lengths.  Use the fact that any two segments tangent to a circle from a common endpoint are congruent to find the missing side lengths.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to circle $O$ at point $C$. 

**What is the length of $\overline{AC}$?**  
[[☃ input-number 1]] units



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Because $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to the circle at point $C$, the radius going to point $C$ is perpendicular to the tangent line.  This means that the measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

We can use the Pythagorean theorem to solve for the length of $\overline {AC}$.

We will need to know the length of $\overline{AO}$.

We know that $\blue{AB = 2}$.  $\purple{BO = 3}$ because it is a radius of the circle.  

$AO = \blue{AB} + \purple{BO}$  
$AO = \blue{2} + \purple{3}$  
$AO = 5$

Now that we know the length of $AO$, we can solve for $AC$.

$\begin{align}
\green{AC}^2 + \pink{OC}^2 &= AO^2\\
\green{AC}^2+ \pink{3}^2 &= 5^2 \\
\green{AC}^2 + \pink{9} &= 25 \\
\green{AC}^2 &= 16\\
\green{AC} &= 4
\end{align}$
The length of $\overline{AC}$ is $4$ units.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a line that could potentially be tangent to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Side A O is broken into two line segments, A B and B O. Segment A B is two units. Segment O C is three units.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to circle $O$ at point $C$. 

**What is the length of $\overline{AC}$?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] units



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Because $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to the circle at point $C$, the radius going to point $C$ is perpendicular to the tangent line.  This means that the measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

We can use the Pythagorean theorem to solve for the length of $\overline {AC}$.

We will need to know the length of $\overline{AO}$.

We know that $\blue{AB = 8}$.  $\purple{BO = 5}$ because it is a radius of the circle.  

$AO = \blue{AB} + \purple{BO}$  
$AO = \blue{8} + \purple{5}$  
$AO = 13$

Now that we know the length of $AO$, we can solve for $AC$.

$\begin{align}
\green{AC}^2 + \pink{OC}^2 &= AO^2\\
\green{AC}^2+ \pink{5}^2 &= 13^2 \\
\green{AC}^2 + \pink{25} &= 169 \\
\green{AC}^2 &= 144\\
\green{AC} &= 12
\end{align}$
The length of $\overline{AC}$ is $12$ units.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a line that could potentially be tangent to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Side A O is broken into two line segments, A B and B O. Segment A B is eight units. Segment O C is five units.

assessment_items.question_content:
$\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to circle $O$ at point $C$. 

**What is the length of $\overline{AB}$?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Because $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to the circle at point $C$, the radius going to point $C$ is perpendicular to the tangent line.  This means that the measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

We can use the Pythagorean theorem to solve for the length of $\overline {AO}$.


$\begin{align}
\green{AC}^2 + \pink{OC}^2 &= AO^2\\
\green{24}^2+ \pink{7}^2 &= AO^2 \\
625&= AO^2\\
25 &= AO\\
\end{align}$

We still need to know the length of $\overline{AB}$.

We know that $AO=25$.  $\purple{BO = 7}$ because it is a radius of the circle.  

>$25 = \blue{AB} + \purple{BO}$  
>$25 = \blue{AB} + \purple{7}$
The length of $\overline{AB}$ is $18$ units.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a line that could potentially be tangent to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Side A O is broken into two line segments, A B and B O. Segment A C is twenty-four units. Segment O C is seven units.

answers.message: This is the length of AO.  What is left after you subtract the radius?

assessment_items.question_content:
$\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to circle $O$ at point $C$. 

**What is the length of $\overline{AB}$?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units



[[☃ image 1]]
hints: Because $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$ is tangent to the circle at point $C$, the radius going to point $C$ is perpendicular to the tangent line.  This means that the measure of $\angle ACO$ is $90^\circ$.

We can use the Pythagorean theorem to solve for the length of $\overline {AO}$.


$\begin{align}
\green{AC}^2 + \pink{OC}^2 &= AO^2\\
\green{15}^2+ \pink{8}^2 &=  AO^2 \\
289 &=  AO^2\\
17 &= AO\\
\end{align}$

We still need to know the length of $\overline{AB}$.

We know that $AO=17$.  $\purple{BO = 8}$ because it is a radius of the circle.  

>$AO = \blue{AB} + \purple{BO}$  
>$17= \blue{AB} + \purple{8}$
The length of $\overline{AB}$ is $9$ units.

options.alt: A circle centered around point O. Segment O C is a radius of the circle. Point A lies outside the circle, and line A C is a line that could potentially be tangent to circle O. A line segment connects point A to point O and intersects the circle at point B. Line segment A O, line segment O C, and line A C create the triangle A O C. Side A O is broken into two line segments, A B and B O. Segment A C is fifteen units. Segment O C is eight units.

answers.message: This is the length of AO.  What is left after you subtract the radius?
